title: Reading and Use of English - Part 6
description: You are going to read an article from which six sentences have been removed. Choose from the sentences A–G the one which fits each gap (37–42). There is one extra sentence which you do not need to use.

content.title: Stars Above the City: Urban Stargazing and Light Pollution
content.text: Stand outside on a clear night in any major city and you will be lucky to see more than a dozen stars. Drive an hour into the countryside and thousands more appear. The difference is light pollution — the excessive and misdirected artificial light that has transformed the night sky over much of the inhabited world. (37) [37] Astronomers estimate that more than eighty percent of the world's population now lives under skies too bright to see the Milky Way. [/37] || Light pollution is not merely an inconvenience for stargazers. Ecologists have documented severe impacts on nocturnal wildlife: migratory birds become disoriented by lit buildings, sea turtle hatchlings head inland toward artificial light rather than seaward toward the moon, and insect populations near lit streets decline measurably. (38) [38] The disruption to natural light-dark cycles affects virtually every organism that evolved under them. [/38] || For astronomers, both professional and amateur, the problem is acute. Most of the great observatories built in the nineteenth century — in Paris, London, Washington — have been rendered essentially useless for serious research by urban expansion. (39) [39] New professional observatories are now located in remote deserts or on high mountaintops specifically to escape light-polluted lower atmospheres. [/39] || The good news is that light pollution is one of the most reversible forms of environmental damage. Replacing inefficient, upward-pointing streetlights with shielded, downward-pointing LED fixtures can reduce sky brightness dramatically without reducing visibility at street level. (40) [40] Several cities that have adopted such programmes report significant improvements in night-sky quality within just a few years. [/40] || 'Dark sky' designations are growing in number. International Dark-Sky Parks and Reserves attract visitors specifically to experience naturally dark nights. (41) [41] In some rural economies, astrotourism has become a significant source of income, providing a compelling economic argument for limiting light pollution. [/41] || Amateur astronomy clubs in cities have responded creatively to the challenge. Many focus on objects bright enough to observe despite light pollution — the Moon, planets, double stars and star clusters. (42) [42] The experience of seeing Saturn's rings through a telescope for the first time, even from a city park, rarely fails to inspire a sense of wonder. [/42]

paragraphs.A: The transition to better lighting technology represents one of the simplest and most cost-effective environmental interventions available.
paragraphs.B: These places offer a reminder of what the night sky looked like before electrification transformed it.
paragraphs.C: They moved to increasingly remote locations as city growth surrounded their original sites.
paragraphs.D: Light pollution, like noise pollution, is a problem that is easy to overlook precisely because it accumulates gradually.
paragraphs.E: This scale of sky brightness loss represents one of the most dramatic changes to the human sensory environment in history.
paragraphs.F: They argue that urban stargazing, though limited, can still be a gateway into a lifelong passion for astronomy.
paragraphs.G: The human health impacts are also significant, since exposure to artificial light at night disrupts circadian rhythms and has been linked to sleep disorders.

questions.explanation: E califica la pérdida de brillo del cielo como uno de los cambios más dramáticos en el entorno sensorial humano, apoyando el impacto de la contaminación lumínica introducida.
G añade los impactos en la salud humana por la exposición a luz artificial nocturna, ampliando la lista de efectos negativos más allá de la fauna.
C explica que los observatorios se trasladaron a lugares cada vez más remotos conforme el crecimiento urbano los rodeó, apoyando la idea de que los observatorios históricos quedaron inutilizados.
A afirma que la transición a mejor tecnología de iluminación es una de las intervenciones medioambientales más simples y rentables, apoyando los resultados positivos de los cambios de iluminación.
B describe estos lugares como recordatorios del cielo nocturno antes de la electrificación, conectando con la descripción de los Parques de Cielo Oscuro.
F argumenta que la observación urbana puede ser una puerta de entrada a la astronomía, apoyando la respuesta creativa de los clubes de astronomía aficionada en las ciudades.
